title: Google I/O 2025 – YYMt7Ddzz6Y
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] JAN KLEINERT: Hi I'm Jan. CECILIA ABADIE: And I'm Cecilia. JAN KLEINERT: We're
here to talk to you about using Compose to
build immersive experiences for Android XR, and some of
the exciting opportunities that lie at the
intersection of AI and XR. Last December, at
the XR Unlocked event alongside Samsung
and Qualcomm, we shared our vision for Android
XR, Android's expansion into extended reality, bringing
a whole spectrum of experiences from virtual reality
to augmented reality to a variety of form factors,
and laid the foundation with the developer preview
for the Android XR SDK. Today, we're excited to revisit
that foundation, covering a quick Android XR
overview, and then building upon it by sharing some
powerful features that leverage your existing Android and
Jetpack Compose skills to build unique XR experiences. We'll also dive into how you
can harness the power of AI directly within your
immersive experiences, opening up a whole new
dimension of possibilities. We'll bring this all together
with a use case deep-dive that showcases an app whose spatial
UI is built with Jetpack Compose for XR, and then uses
the Gemini 2.0 Flash and Imagen 3 models in Vertex
AI for Firebase to do some virtual AI
powered home decorating. Then we'll wrap things up
by sharing some resources and details on how
you can get started building apps at the
intersection of AI and extended reality. Cecilia, I'll hand
it over to you to get us started with an
overview of Android XR. CECILIA ABADIE: Thank you, Jan. History shows that it's within
these type of intersections that innovation flourishes. So we are super excited
to explore it with you. Android XR is the first platform
built for the Gemini era, and we believe AI
has the potential to revolutionize immersive
experiences, making them more intelligent, personalized,
intuitive, and agentic. Unlike traditional
devices, XR devices have cameras and
sensors that enable your apps to perceive the user
and their environment naturally.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YYMt7Ddzz6Y
text:
to revolutionize immersive
experiences, making them more intelligent, personalized,
intuitive, and agentic. Unlike traditional
devices, XR devices have cameras and
sensors that enable your apps to perceive the user
and their environment naturally. This offers a unique
opportunity for AI. Imagine applications that can
understand your surroundings, respond to your natural
language in a spatial context, and even generate
content dynamically based on your environment
and preferences. All of these built with Google's
leading privacy standards by design. Let's start with an
overview of Android XR. We believe extended
reality, blending the physical and virtual worlds
will transform how we learn, connect, create, and play. Across a variety
of use cases, we've been imagining a
future where computing goes beyond the traditional
rectangular screens, where content appears
in physical space and reach applications truly
understand your environment. Extending reality in
helpful and innovative ways. Combined with multimodal
AI enabled directly into the system, Android XR
opens up opportunities for you to build a wide
range of experiences beyond what's possible
on devices today. This new computing
platform levels up experiences to
help users navigate increasingly complex information
in a more seamless, intuitive, and visual way. And to expand their world
with endless possibilities across a spectrum
of form factors. Starting from headsets, we
see immersive experiences that surround you
and in the future, glasses that bring helpful
in-the-moment intelligent insights to users
day-to-day life. The first Android XR
device will be a headset, Project Moohan from Samsung. Starting from its launch
later this year, the ecosystem will expand further across
headsets and glasses. Now, because Android XR is
an extension of the Android ecosystem, users can install a
wide range of apps on headsets from the Play Store. Many of you have already been
optimizing your existing apps for large screens.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YYMt7Ddzz6Y
text:
Now, because Android XR is
an extension of the Android ecosystem, users can install a
wide range of apps on headsets from the Play Store. Many of you have already been
optimizing your existing apps for large screens. To learn more about how these
same optimizations will also help your apps look
great on Android XR, be sure to check out this talk
about adaptive app development. Let's take a minute to look at
Home Space versus Full Space experiences on XR headsets. When you first put on a headset,
you are welcomed by Home Space. Home Space enables
you to multitask multiple two dimensional
application Windows side by side. For apps that have implemented
XR differentiated features, you can transition the
app into Full Space, where other apps are hidden. And the Full Space
app comes into focus, filling the entire
view around you. When you are ready to build
the XR differentiated apps, whether you are an
Android developer using Android Studio or an
immersive app developer using tools like Unity, we want
to support you with the tools and technologies that you
love and you're already building with today. Now I'm going to
hand it back to Jan to explain more about the
spatial UI capabilities and Jetpack Compose for XR. JAN KLEINERT: Thanks, Cecilia. Jetpack Compose for XR is one
of the libraries that make up the Jetpack XR SDK that we
launched in developer preview last December. Our goal with this
SDK was to provide a comprehensive and
developer friendly toolkit for building immersive
experiences on Android XR headsets. Building user interfaces for XR
comes with both opportunities and challenges. You have the opportunity
to take advantage of the unconstrained
boundaries and the ability to decompose your apps into
multiple spatial panels with depth. While one of the
key challenges in XR is creating user interfaces that
feel natural and comfortable across different spatial
contexts and device capabilities. Jetpack Compose for XR
aims to make this easier, so you can declaratively build
your spatial UI and layout using
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YYMt7Ddzz6Y
text:
is creating user interfaces that
feel natural and comfortable across different spatial
contexts and device capabilities. Jetpack Compose for XR
aims to make this easier, so you can declaratively build
your spatial UI and layout using familiar Compose concepts such
as rows and columns adapted to suit the 3D space in
which you're building. Because Compose
for XR is working with spatial layouts
and measurements that exist in three dimensions
rather than the two dimensions you may be
used to working with, there are some differences. Before we begin looking at
the different spatial UI components that
are available, it's important to understand
the concept of a subspace and how that relates to
spatial UI components. A subspace is a partition
of 3D space within your app, where you can build 3D spatial
layouts, place 3D models, or add depth to otherwise
two dimensional content. A subspace and its
contents are only rendered when
spatialization is enabled. That is, only when your app is
running on an XR enabled device and in Full Space mode. When your app is in
Home Space, or if it's running on a non-XR
enabled device, any code within that
subspace is ignored. A subspace can be
placed anywhere within your app's UI hierarchy. This can help you share things
like existing app architecture between XR and
other form factors, without needing to
re-architect your whole app. There are two categories
of spatial UI components in Android XR. Subspace composables,
which can only be rendered within a subspace,
and other spatialized components that do not have to be
called within a subspace. One of the most foundational
subspace composables is the spatial panel. You can think of a spatial
panel as a plane in 3D space, with a width and a height that
acts as a surface or container in which content
can be displayed. Spatial panels are the
fundamental building blocks of Android XR apps,
and they serve as containers for UI elements,
interactive components, and immersive content. The spatial panel
you see here is
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YYMt7Ddzz6Y
text:
in which content
can be displayed. Spatial panels are the
fundamental building blocks of Android XR apps,
and they serve as containers for UI elements,
interactive components, and immersive content. The spatial panel
you see here is part of the spatial
AI artwork app that will be referencing
throughout the talk. Because a spatial panel
is a subspace composable, you must call it
within a subspace, as you can see illustrated
in this example. Similar to how you use modifiers
in Compose when you work with typical 2D composables, in XR,
you use a subspace modifier. Well, the concept is similar. They aren't interchangeable. When you work with a
subspace composable, you use a subspace modifier. Some subspace
modifiers will sound familiar, like padding,
width or height, while others are specific to
XR, like movable or resizable. For this spatial
panel, for instance, we're using a subspace
modifier to change the size and behavior of the panel. If you're already familiar
with Compose layout, such as building with
rows and columns, then you'll find that
laying out spatial UI with multiple spatial
panels feels intuitive. Let's consider spatial row and
spatial column and how they compare to their 2D
counterparts row and column. Row and column are used to place
items horizontally or vertically on a screen. That is in 2D space. But in XR, we're able to
build layouts and user interfaces in three dimensions. So these spatial row and spatial
column composables for XR allow you to arrange elements linearly
in 3D space along a specified axis. This makes it straightforward
to create structured UI elements that maintain
their relative positioning in the immersive environment. Now let's look at some of
those other spatialized UI components that don't have to
be placed within a subspace. These components consist of
conventional 2D elements wrapped in a spatial container, and
they can be placed anywhere in your app's UI hierarchy. When spatialization
is not enabled, their spatialized features will
be ignored and they'll fall back
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YYMt7Ddzz6Y
text:
These components consist of
conventional 2D elements wrapped in a spatial container, and
they can be placed anywhere in your app's UI hierarchy. When spatialization
is not enabled, their spatialized features will
be ignored and they'll fall back to their 2D counterparts. Orbiters are floating elements
that typically contain controls the user can interact with. Orbiters are anchored to spatial
panels or other entities, such as spatial layouts. Orbiters can help
declutter your app's UI and bring the user's attention
to important controls, such as a navigation rail,
like you see in this example. By wrapping the navigation
rail with an orbiter, it extracts it from
your 2D layout. From here, you'd
specify where and how you'd like to attach
it to your app's panel. Adding elevation to
spatial UI components can help get a user's attention,
creates better hierarchy, and can improve legibility. Spatial dialogues
and spatial popups are two types of
spatialized components that can help add
depth and elevation to your app in meaningful ways. Spatial dialogues provide a way
to present important information or prompts to the user in a
focused and modal manner within the 3D environment. When spatialization is
enabled, the dialogue will be placed higher in Z-depth
to display an elevated dialogue and scrim panels. Spatial popups behave similarly. And when you want even
more fine-grained control over elevation in your
UI, spatial elevation allows you to elevate any
composable in your app to a higher Z-depth
than the spatial panel. Up until now, we've
been looking at how you can build and
customize spatial UI and content for your apps
using Jetpack Compose for XR. In addition, material design
provides components and layouts that adapt for XR. You can adapt your current
material three implementation by adding the
EnableXrComponentOverrides wrapper. You can use this
wrapper with components such as navigation
rail or navigation bar, and those components
will automatically adapt to an orbiter if
the app is in Full Space.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YYMt7Ddzz6Y
text:
by adding the
EnableXrComponentOverrides wrapper. You can use this
wrapper with components such as navigation
rail or navigation bar, and those components
will automatically adapt to an orbiter if
the app is in Full Space. This is the approach we
took for spatializing the UI for the spatial
artwork example app. Using this wrapper results
in the navigation rail moving into an orbiter when
the app is in Full Space. Beyond UI, we're also
focusing on making it easier for you to take advantage of
the unique capabilities of XR headsets to include content like
stereoscopic video in your apps using Compose for XR. Because of the nature
of XR headsets, where each eye has
its own display, this form factor is
particularly well suited for viewing
stereoscopic content. Stereoscopic video,
sometimes called 3D video, creates the illusion of 3D depth
by showing slightly different images or streams of
content to each eye. Much like what happens
when we view things in the real world with
our own eyes, these two different images, or
streams of content, are combined by our brain
to produce that single image with the perception of depth. Each image in a
stereoscopic video is encoded as two eyes,
either in a top/bottom format, as shown here, or in
a side by side format. We're excited to introduce
SpatialExternalSurface, a new composable
that can support rendering both stereoscopic and
monoscopic, or regular video formats. While some of the concepts
we've covered may be new to you, remember that you don't
have to start from scratch, and you don't need to specialize
every part of your app. You can build off your existing
investments in large screen and adaptive development to
expand your reach to XR devices, and then implement whatever
spatial UI features are most relevant and
useful to your app. Now, I'm going to turn it
over to Cecilia to cover the power of AI in Android XR. CECILIA ABADIE: Amazing. We are excited to
talk to you today about one of the ultimate
frontiers in Android
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YYMt7Ddzz6Y
text:
most relevant and
useful to your app. Now, I'm going to turn it
over to Cecilia to cover the power of AI in Android XR. CECILIA ABADIE: Amazing. We are excited to
talk to you today about one of the ultimate
frontiers in Android XR, the convergence of these
two fast-moving waves, extended reality and artificial
intelligence. So what are some
of the use cases that are uniquely positioned
to take advantage of AI in Android XR? First, creative
content generation, the use case that we'll
explore in this talk with Jan. AI can generate images and
interactive elements on the fly, and present this
generated content in ways that take advantage
of XR spatial capabilities, including spatial anchoring
and infinite space. Second, context
aware interactions. AI can analyze the
user's environment and provide relevant
information or actions that enable the user to interact with
the physical and digital content in novel ways. And third, one of my favorites,
assistance and coaching, where conversational
agents guide users answer questions
and personalize immersive environments to tailor
them to user's preferences, behaviors, and
accessibility needs. This includes building real-time
conversational multimodal agents able to process, text,
voice, image, and video inputs and outputs. To match the different levels
of your AI requirements, Gemini offers a wide range
of constantly evolving foundational pre-trained models. You can mix these
models however you like within your application,
from the largest and most capable to the lighter
and faster ones. You also have the ability
to train and fine tune your own custom models
for tasks such as image recognition, natural language
processing, and generative AI. You have different
options to start leveraging the power
of generative AI directly in your Android
XR applications today. You can use Vertex
AI in Firebase, which offers both an Android
SDK in general availability and a new Unity SDK that
is in public preview. You also have the option to
use Vertex AI APIs directly,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YYMt7Ddzz6Y
text:
directly in your Android
XR applications today. You can use Vertex
AI in Firebase, which offers both an Android
SDK in general availability and a new Unity SDK that
is in public preview. You also have the option to
use Vertex AI APIs directly, if you prefer to implement your
AI features on the backend. All of these options
provide seamless ways to integrate Google's cutting
edge AI models and APIs into your XR applications. Over to you, Jan, to bring
this all together with an XR AI example. JAN KLEINERT: OK, let's
dive into how all of this can work together to do
some virtual AI powered home decorating. Imagine an app on Android
XR built with the Jetpack XR SDK that allows users to create
personalized virtual spatial artwork using AI. Here's how it works. Wearing my Android XR
headset and Passthrough mode, I can look around
my physical room. I have this blank wall here
in the room that could really use some decoration. Using the Camera
app on my headset, I'm taking a few
pictures of the room. Before I implemented
this in the app, I drafted and validated the
prompt in Vertex AI Studio using the Gemini 2.0 Flash model
by supplying both a text prompt and an image of the room. Here's the prompt I decided on. Generate a list of the primary
colors in the room that would be best to match with artwork. List only the color names and
HEX values in this format. Once I was satisfied with
the prompt and responses, then I implemented this
in my app using Vertex AI and Firebase. Based on this color analysis,
then I used the Imagen 3 model to generate a few abstract
images representing pieces of artwork that could
coordinate nicely with the colors in my room. Again, I use Vertex AI Studio
but with the Imagen 3 model this time to validate the prompt
and see what kind of results would be generated. And then in the
app, I implemented this prompt with
Vertex AI in Firebase using the Imagen
3 model this time. By default, this would have
returned a single image, but since we wanted four
options to choose from,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YYMt7Ddzz6Y
text:
And then in the
app, I implemented this prompt with
Vertex AI in Firebase using the Imagen
3 model this time. By default, this would have
returned a single image, but since we wanted four
options to choose from, I used the generation
config, that you see here, to specify the number of images
that I wanted in the response. Now let's take a look. I can see all four images
that were generated for me. And when I decide
on one that I like, I can select the Painting
icon to get a movable painting that I can attach to the
wall where I want it. Attaching a panel or other
entity to a wall like this is called anchoring. In this case, you can see
there's a little Lock icon. That's going to persist or store
the information about where this painting is anchored. In effect, remembering
where I've placed it. Now I can select my favorite
piece of generated artwork and anchor it to a wall
in my physical environment while the app remains
in Passthrough mode. You can use the ARCore for
Jetpack XR library, which is part of the Jetpack XR SDK to
implement this kind of anchoring behavior. You can learn about anchoring
in depth in the talk on building differentiated apps for
Android XR with 3D content. My teammate Amy got in on the
virtual decorating fun too. Here she is trying it out. I really love the
color combinations in the artwork that were
generated for this room. This app just
scratches the surface of what you can build when
you combine the expressive UI capabilities of Compose for
XR, the spatial awareness of the Android XR platform,
and the creative potential of these AI models through
Vertex AI and Firebase. Think about the possibilities. Personalized virtual home decor,
context-aware storytelling, immersive educational
experiences that adapt to the
learning environment. The potential is
truly limitless. We are incredibly excited
about the future of Android XR, and we can't wait to see what
you, our talented developer community, will create
with these new tools. If you haven't
already, we recommend
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YYMt7Ddzz6Y
text:
The potential is
truly limitless. We are incredibly excited
about the future of Android XR, and we can't wait to see what
you, our talented developer community, will create
with these new tools. If you haven't
already, we recommend you check out the
announcement blog post and watch some of the recordings
from our developer preview launch. Check out our
developer documentation for the Android XR SDK, where
you can also find instructions for installing the Android XR
Emulator, samples for Android XR, and documentation
and samples for Vertex AI and Firebase to help
you bring those AI use cases to life in XR. Download Android Studio
with the Android XR Emulator and get started
building for XR today. The advancements in
the Android XR SDK, combined with the power of
AI, are opening up a new era of immersive computing. Your creativity and innovation
will drive this future, and we're here to
provide you with the best possible tools and resources. Thank you for
joining this session. [MUSIC PLAYING]
